title: L07 · Object Pronouns & Gustar-type Verbs
objective: Master the small but mighty Spanish object-pronoun system: me, te, lo/la, le, nos, os, los/las, les, se. Then the famous 'gustar' construction that flips the subject and object compared to English/French.
strategy: Two parts: (1) the pronoun tables — direct (the thing being acted on) vs indirect (to whom). (2) the GUSTAR construction — Spanish doesn't say 'I like X'; it says 'X is pleasing TO me' (Me gusta X). This is HUGE; many learners stumble here for months. French speakers: it's identical to 'plaire' (Cela me plaît).

sections.title: Direct object pronouns (DO) — me, te, lo, la, nos, os, los, las
sections.note: Replaces the noun receiving the action ('I see HIM', 'I read IT'). Goes BEFORE the verb.

blocks.text: Direct objects: me / te / lo / la / nos / os / los / las
blocks.audioText: me ve, te veo, lo veo, la veo, nos ven, os ven, los veo, las veo
blocks.rule: Position: BEFORE the conjugated verb. 'Lo veo' = I see him/it. 'Te quiero' = I love you. With infinitives/gerunds you can attach the pronoun to the end ('Quiero verte' = I want to see you).

examples.audioText: Ves el libro? Sí, lo veo.
examples.translation: Do you see the book? — Yes, I see it. / Tu vois le livre? — Oui, je le vois.

examples.audioText: Conoces a María? Sí, la conozco.
examples.translation: Do you know Maria? — Yes, I know her. / Tu connais Maria? — Oui, je la connais.
I love you. / Je t'aime.
They invite us to the party. / Ils nous invitent à la fête.

blocks.text: With infinitive or gerund: pronoun BEFORE the conjugated verb OR attached to the END
blocks.audioText: Quiero verlo. Lo quiero ver. Estoy haciéndolo. Lo estoy haciendo.
blocks.rule: Both placements are correct. 'Quiero verlo' = 'Lo quiero ver' (I want to see him). Note: when you attach to the end of a longer word, you may need to ADD an accent to keep stress in place ('haciendo' → 'haciéndolo').

examples.translation: I want to see you tomorrow. / Je veux te voir demain.
(Same meaning, different placement.)

sections.title: Indirect object pronouns (IO) — me, te, le, nos, os, les
sections.note: Replaces the person TO WHOM something is done. 'I give the book TO HIM' → 'Le doy el libro'.

blocks.text: Indirect objects: me / te / le / nos / os / les
blocks.audioText: me das, te doy, le doy, nos das, os doy, les doy
blocks.rule: Same as direct objects EXCEPT 3rd person: le (sing.) and les (plur.). Use IO when there's an implicit 'to/for someone'. 'Le digo la verdad' = I tell HIM/HER the truth (literally: to him/her).

examples.text: Le doy el regalo a María.
examples.audioText: Le doy el regalo a María.
examples.translation: I give the gift to Maria. / Je donne le cadeau à Maria.
examples.ipa: 'le' refers to 'a María'. The 'a María' part is optional clarification — 'Le doy el regalo' is enough.

examples.translation: I'll write you an email. / Je t'écris un e-mail.

examples.text: Les digo la verdad a mis padres.
examples.audioText: Les digo la verdad a mis padres.
examples.translation: I tell my parents the truth. / Je dis la vérité à mes parents.

sections.title: When both DO and IO appear together
sections.note: Two pronouns in a row — the order is INDIRECT first, DIRECT second. And: 'le/les' magically becomes 'se' before lo/la/los/las.

blocks.text: Order: IO before DO. Both go before the verb.
blocks.audioText: Me lo das. Te lo digo. Nos lo dan.
blocks.rule: Pattern: [indirect][direct][verb]. Reverse of English where the order is flexible.

examples.translation: You give it to me. / Tu me le donnes.
I'll tell it to you. / Je te le dis.
They bring them (fem) to us. / Ils nous les apportent.

blocks.audioText: Le lo doy → Se lo doy.
blocks.rule: Spanish doesn't allow two L-sound pronouns next to each other. So 'le lo' becomes 'se lo', and 'les lo' becomes 'se lo'. The 'se' here means 'to him/her/them' — context tells you which.

examples.translation: I give it to Maria. / Je le donne à Maria.
examples.ipa: Would have been 'le lo doy' but that's forbidden — becomes 'se lo'.

examples.translation: I'll tell him/her it tomorrow. / Je le lui dis demain.

sections.title: Gustar — 'I like X' = 'X is pleasing TO me'
sections.note: THE classic Spanish construction. The thing you like is the SUBJECT; you are the indirect object. French speakers: identical to 'plaire' — Cela me plaît. English speakers: this needs to be UNLEARNED.

blocks.audioText: Me gusta el café. Me gustan los libros.
blocks.rule: Step by step: (1) WHO likes it → indirect object pronoun (me, te, le, nos, os, les). (2) WHAT they like → subject of gustar. (3) Verb is 'gusta' if subject is singular, 'gustan' if plural. (4) Yes, the subject typically comes AFTER the verb.

examples.translation: I like coffee. (lit. coffee is pleasing to me) / J'aime le café. (Le café me plaît.)
I like books. / J'aime les livres. — note 'gustan' because 'libros' is plural
Do you like chocolate? / Tu aimes le chocolat?
She likes to dance. / Elle aime danser.
examples.ipa: 'A ella' clarifies who 'le' refers to. The subject is 'bailar' (infinitive = singular subject).

examples.translation: We like music. / Nous aimons la musique.

blocks.text: Optional 'A + person' clarifier for emphasis or contrast
blocks.audioText: A mí me gusta. A ti te gusta. A él le gusta.
blocks.rule: Spanish often adds 'a + pronoun/name' to clarify or emphasise who likes. 'A mí me gusta' = 'I (emphasised) like'. Always KEEP the regular IO pronoun — the 'a + ...' is just extra.

examples.text: A mí me gusta el café.
examples.audioText: A mí me gusta el café.
examples.translation: I like coffee. (emphasised) / Moi, j'aime le café.

examples.text: A María le gustan los gatos.
examples.audioText: A María le gustan los gatos.
examples.translation: Maria likes cats. / Maria aime les chats.

examples.text: A nosotros nos gusta el cine.
examples.audioText: A nosotros nos gusta el cine.
examples.translation: We like cinema. / Nous, nous aimons le cinéma.

blocks.text: Strength levels: encantar > gustar mucho > gustar > no gustar mucho > no gustar > odiar
blocks.audioText: Me encanta el café. Me gusta mucho el café. Me gusta el café. No me gusta el café. Odio el café.
blocks.rule: ENCANTAR works like gustar but means 'to love' (as in 'I really like'). NEVER use 'mucho' with 'encantar' — it's already at maximum. 'odiar' (to hate) is a normal verb, NOT a gustar-type.

examples.translation: I love chocolate. / J'adore le chocolat.
I like chocolate a lot. / J'aime beaucoup le chocolat.
I hate spinach. / Je déteste les épinards. — note: 'yo' as subject, not the gustar pattern

sections.title: Other verbs that work LIKE gustar
sections.note: About 20 high-frequency Spanish verbs follow the gustar pattern. Memorise the family.

blocks.audioText: Me duele la cabeza. Me duelen los pies.
blocks.rule: Spanish doesn't say 'I hurt my head' — it says 'my head hurts to me' (Me duele la cabeza). The body part is the subject.

examples.translation: I have a headache. (lit. my head hurts me) / J'ai mal à la tête.
My feet hurt. / J'ai mal aux pieds. — plural 'duelen'

blocks.audioText: Me falta dinero. Me faltan dos euros.
blocks.rule: 'Me falta X' = 'I lack X' or 'I'm missing X'. Same backwards structure as gustar.

examples.translation: I don't have enough time. (lit. time is lacking to me) / Le temps me manque.
You're 2 euros short. / Il te manque 2 euros.

blocks.audioText: Me interesa la historia. Me interesan los idiomas.

examples.translation: Politics interests me. / La politique m'intéresse.

blocks.rule: 'Me parece X' = 'It seems X to me' / 'I think X'.

examples.translation: It seems like a good idea to me. / Cela me semble une bonne idée.
What do you think? / Qu'en penses-tu?

blocks.text: quedar — to suit / fit (clothes), to be left
blocks.audioText: Te queda bien. Me quedan tres minutos.

examples.text: Te queda muy bien ese vestido.
examples.audioText: Te queda muy bien ese vestido.
examples.translation: That dress suits you very well. / Cette robe te va très bien.
I have 5 euros left. / Il me reste 5 euros.
